[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/64/Fachada_do_Pal%C3%A1cio_do_Planalto_-_49727772326.jpg/250px-Fachada_do_Pal%C3%A1cio_do_Planalto_-_49727772326.jpg)](/wiki/File:Fachada_do_Pal%C3%A1cio_do_Planalto_-_49727772326.jpg)

[Palácio do Planalto](/wiki/Pal%C3%A1cio_do_Planalto "Palácio do Planalto"), the official workplace of the [President of Brazil](/wiki/President_of_Brazil "President of Brazil")

The form of government is a [democratic](/wiki/Democracy "Democracy") [federative](/wiki/Federation "Federation") [republic](/wiki/Republic "Republic"), with a [presidential system](/wiki/Presidential_system "Presidential system").[[21]](#cite_note-Constituição-25) The president is both head of state and head of government of the Union and is elected for a four-year term,[[21]](#cite_note-Constituição-25) with the possibility of re-election for a second successive term. The current president is [Luiz Inácio Lula da Silva](/wiki/Luiz_In%C3%A1cio_Lula_da_Silva "Luiz Inácio Lula da Silva").[[223]](#cite_note-227) The president appoints the [Ministers of State](/wiki/Minister_of_State "Minister of State"), who form the [cabinet](/wiki/Cabinet_of_Brazil "Cabinet of Brazil") and assist in government.[[21]](#cite_note-Constituição-25)

Legislative houses in each political entity are the main source of law in Brazil. The [National Congress](/wiki/National_Congress_of_Brazil "National Congress of Brazil") is the federation's bicameral legislature, consisting of the [Chamber of Deputies](/wiki/Chamber_of_Deputies_of_Brazil "Chamber of Deputies of Brazil") and the [Federal Senate](/wiki/Senate_of_Brazil "Senate of Brazil"). The Senate represents the [26 states](/wiki/States_of_Brazil "States of Brazil") and the [Federal District](/wiki/Federal_District_(Brazil) "Federal District (Brazil)"). Each state and the Federal District has a representation of three senators, who are elected by popular ballot for a term of eight years.[[224]](#cite_note-228)[[225]](#cite_note-229) The Chamber of Deputies represents the people of each state, and its members are elected for a four-year term by a system of [proportional representation](/wiki/Proportional_representation "Proportional representation").[[226]](#cite_note-230) Judiciary authorities exercise jurisdictional duties almost exclusively.

According to [International IDEA](/wiki/International_Institute_for_Democracy_and_Electoral_Assistance "International Institute for Democracy and Electoral Assistance")’s Global State of Democracy (GSoD) Indices and Democracy Tracker, Brazil performs in the mid to high range on overall democratic measures, with particular strengths in inclusive suffrage, freedom of religion, and civic engagement.[[227]](#cite_note-231) In 2021, the [Economist Intelligence Unit](/wiki/Economist_Intelligence_Unit "Economist Intelligence Unit")'s [Democracy Index](/wiki/Democracy_Index "Democracy Index") categorized Brazil as a "[flawed democracy](/wiki/Flawed_democracy "Flawed democracy")", ranking 46th in the report,[[228]](#cite_note-232) and [Freedom House](/wiki/Freedom_House "Freedom House") classified it as a [free country](/wiki/Free_World "Free World") at *[Freedom in the World](/wiki/Freedom_in_the_World "Freedom in the World")* report.[[229]](#cite_note-FITW-TG-2021-233) According to the [Regimes of the World](/wiki/V-Dem_Democracy_Indices#Regimes_of_the_World "V-Dem Democracy Indices") classification Brazil was an [electoral democracy](/wiki/Representative_democracy "Representative democracy") in year 2024.[[230]](#cite_note-j496-234)

The political-administrative organization of the Federative Republic of Brazil comprises the Union, the states, the Federal District, and the municipalities.[[21]](#cite_note-Constituição-25) The Union, the states, the Federal District, and the municipalities, are the "spheres of government". The federation is set on five fundamental principles: sovereignty, citizenship, dignity of human beings, the social values of labor and freedom of enterprise, and political [pluralism](/wiki/Pluralism_(political_theory) "Pluralism (political theory)").[[21]](#cite_note-Constituição-25)

The classic tripartite branches of government (executive, legislative and judicial under a checks and balances system) are formally established by the constitution.[[21]](#cite_note-Constituição-25) The executive and legislative are organized [independently in all three spheres of government](/wiki/Separation_of_powers "Separation of powers"), while the judiciary is organized only at the federal and state and Federal District spheres. All members of the executive and legislative branches are directly elected.[[231]](#cite_note-embassy-235)[[232]](#cite_note-236)[[233]](#cite_note-237)

For most of its democratic history, Brazil has had a [multi-party system](/wiki/Multi-party_system "Multi-party system"), with [proportional representation](/wiki/Proportional_representation "Proportional representation"). Voting is [compulsory](/wiki/Compulsory_voting "Compulsory voting") for the literate between 18 and 70 years old and optional for illiterates and those between 16 and 18 or beyond 70.[[21]](#cite_note-Constituição-25) The country has around [30 registered political parties](/wiki/List_of_political_parties_in_Brazil "List of political parties in Brazil"). Twenty political parties are represented in Congress. It is common for politicians to switch parties, and thus the proportion of congressional seats held by particular parties changes regularly.[[234]](#cite_note-238)

### Law

Main article: [Law of Brazil](/wiki/Law_of_Brazil "Law of Brazil")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/be/Supremo_Tribunal_Federal2.jpg/250px-Supremo_Tribunal_Federal2.jpg)](/wiki/File:Supremo_Tribunal_Federal2.jpg)

The [Supreme Federal Court of Brazil](/wiki/Supreme_Federal_Court "Supreme Federal Court") serves primarily as the Constitutional Court of the country

Brazilian law is based on the [civil law](/wiki/Civil_law_(legal_system) "Civil law (legal system)") legal system[[235]](#cite_note-239) and [civil law](/wiki/Civil_law_(legal_system) "Civil law (legal system)") concepts prevail over common law practice. Most of Brazilian law is codified, although non-codified statutes also represent a substantial part, playing a complementary role. Court decisions set out interpretive guidelines; however, they are seldom binding on other specific cases. Doctrinal works and the works of academic jurists have strong influence in law creation and in law cases. Judges and other judicial officials are appointed after passing entry exams.[[231]](#cite_note-embassy-235)

The legal system is based on the [Federal Constitution](/wiki/Constitution_of_Brazil "Constitution of Brazil"), promulgated on 5 October 1988, and the fundamental law of Brazil. All other legislation and court decisions must conform to its rules.[[236]](#cite_note-240) As of July 2022[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Brazil&action=edit), there have been 124 amendments.[[237]](#cite_note-241) The highest court is the [Supreme Federal Court](/wiki/Supreme_Federal_Court "Supreme Federal Court"). States have their own constitutions, which must not contradict the Federal Constitution.[[238]](#cite_note-242) Municipalities and the Federal District have "organic laws" (*leis orgânicas*), which act in a similar way to constitutions.[[239]](#cite_note-DeffentiBarral2011-243) Legislative entities are the main source of statutes, although in certain matters judiciary and executive bodies may enact legal norms.[[21]](#cite_note-Constituição-25) Jurisdiction is administered by the judiciary entities, although in rare situations the Federal Constitution allows the Federal Senate to pass on legal judgments.[[21]](#cite_note-Constituição-25) There are also specialized military, labor and [electoral courts](/wiki/Brazilian_Election_Justice "Brazilian Election Justice").[[21]](#cite_note-Constituição-25)

### Political subdivisions

Main article: [Subdivisions of Brazil](/wiki/Subdivisions_of_Brazil "Subdivisions of Brazil")

Further information: [Municipalities of Brazil](/wiki/Municipalities_of_Brazil "Municipalities of Brazil"), [States of Brazil](/wiki/States_of_Brazil "States of Brazil"), and [Regions of Brazil](/wiki/Regions_of_Brazil "Regions of Brazil")

Brazil is a federation composed of 26 [states](/wiki/Federated_state "Federated state"), one [federal district](/wiki/Federal_district "Federal district"), and the 5,571 [municipalities](/wiki/Municipality "Municipality").[[21]](#cite_note-Constituição-25) States have autonomous administrations, collect their own taxes and receive a share of taxes collected by the Federal government. They have a governor and a unicameral legislative body elected directly by their voters. They also have independent [Courts of Law](/wiki/Court_of_Justice_(Brazil) "Court of Justice (Brazil)") for common justice. States have the ability to create their own laws and establish their own constitutions[[240]](#cite_note-caj.fiu.edu-244)[[241]](#cite_note-Forum_of_Federations-245)[[242]](#cite_note-Montgomery-246) but criminal and civil laws can be voted by only the federal bicameral Congress and are uniform throughout the country.[[243]](#cite_note-247)[[21]](#cite_note-Constituição-25)

Municipalities, as the states, have autonomous administrations, collect their own taxes and receive a share of taxes collected by the federal and state government.[[21]](#cite_note-Constituição-25) Each has an elected mayor and legislative body, but no separate Court of Law. Indeed, a Court of Law organized by the state can encompass many municipalities in a single justice administrative division called *[comarca](/wiki/Comarca "Comarca")*.[[21]](#cite_note-Constituição-25)

Brazil's constitution also provides for the creation of [federal territories](/wiki/Federal_territories_of_Brazil "Federal territories of Brazil"), which are administrative divisions directly controlled by the federal government. However, there are currently no federal territories in the country, as the 1988 constitution abolished the last three: Amapá and Roraima (which gained statehood) and Fernando de Noronha, which became a state district of [Pernambuco](/wiki/Pernambuco "Pernambuco").[[244]](#cite_note-248)[[245]](#cite_note-249)

### Foreign relations

Main article: [Foreign relations of Brazil](/wiki/Foreign_relations_of_Brazil "Foreign relations of Brazil")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d9/Itamaraty_%2847946720843%29.jpg/250px-Itamaraty_%2847946720843%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Itamaraty_(47946720843).jpg)

[Itamaraty Palace](/wiki/Itamaraty_Palace "Itamaraty Palace"), the seat of the [Ministry of Foreign Affairs](/wiki/Ministry_of_Foreign_Affairs_(Brazil) "Ministry of Foreign Affairs (Brazil)")

Brazil's international relations are based on Article 4 of the Federal Constitution, which establishes [non-intervention](/wiki/Non-interventionism "Non-interventionism"), [self-determination](/wiki/Self-determination "Self-determination"), [international cooperation](/wiki/Internationalism_(politics) "Internationalism (politics)") and the [peaceful settlement of conflicts](/wiki/Peacebuilding "Peacebuilding") as the guiding principles of Brazil's relationship with other countries and multilateral organizations.[[246]](#cite_note-250) According to the constitution, the president has ultimate authority over foreign policy, while Congress is tasked with reviewing and considering all diplomatic nominations and [international treaties](/wiki/Treaty "Treaty"), as well as legislation relating to Brazilian foreign policy.[[247]](#cite_note-251)

Brazil's foreign policy is a by-product of the country's position as a [regional power](/wiki/Regional_power "Regional power") in Latin America, a leader among [developing countries](/wiki/Developing_countries "Developing countries"), and an emerging [world power](/wiki/World_power "World power").[[248]](#cite_note-252) Brazilian foreign policy has generally been based on the principles of [multilateralism](/wiki/Multilateralism "Multilateralism"), peaceful dispute settlement, and non-intervention in the affairs of other countries.[[249]](#cite_note-253) Brazil is a founding member state of the [Community of Portuguese Language Countries](/wiki/Community_of_Portuguese_Language_Countries "Community of Portuguese Language Countries") (CPLP), also known as the Lusophone Commonwealth, an international organization and political association of [Lusophone](/wiki/Lusophone "Lusophone") nations.

An increasingly well-developed tool of Brazil's foreign policy is providing aid as a donor to other developing countries.[[250]](#cite_note-ODI1-254) Brazil does not just use its growing economic strength to provide financial aid, but it also provides high levels of expertise and most importantly of all, a quiet non-confrontational diplomacy to improve governance levels.[[250]](#cite_note-ODI1-254) Total aid is estimated to be around $1 billion per year.[[250]](#cite_note-ODI1-254) In addition, Brazil already managed a [peacekeeping mission in Haiti](/wiki/United_Nations_Stabilisation_Mission_in_Haiti "United Nations Stabilisation Mission in Haiti") ($350 million) and makes in-kind contributions to the [World Food Programme](/wiki/World_Food_Programme "World Food Programme") ($300 million).[[250]](#cite_note-ODI1-254) The scale of this aid places it on par with China and India.[[250]](#cite_note-ODI1-254) The Brazilian [South-South](/wiki/South-South "South-South") aid has been described as a "global model in waiting".[[251]](#cite_note-255)

### Military

Main article: [Brazilian Armed Forces](/wiki/Brazilian_Armed_Forces "Brazilian Armed Forces")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/7f/Opera%C3%A7%C3%A3o_%22Poseidon_2021%22_%2851475998419%29.jpg/250px-Opera%C3%A7%C3%A3o_%22Poseidon_2021%22_%2851475998419%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Opera%C3%A7%C3%A3o_%22Poseidon_2021%22_(51475998419).jpg)

Brazilian Navy's flagship [PHM *Atlântico*](/wiki/Brazilian_helicopter_carrier_Atl%C3%A2ntico "Brazilian helicopter carrier Atlântico") and [frigate *Liberal* (F-43)](/wiki/Brazilian_frigate_Liberal_(F-43) "Brazilian frigate Liberal (F-43)") (background)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/89/2020_-_Dia_do_Aviador_%2850886895273%29.jpg/250px-2020_-_Dia_do_Aviador_%2850886895273%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:2020_-_Dia_do_Aviador_(50886895273).jpg)

[Saab Gripen NG](/wiki/Saab_JAS_39_Gripen#Brazil "Saab JAS 39 Gripen") of the [Brazilian Air Force](/wiki/Brazilian_Air_Force "Brazilian Air Force")

The armed forces of Brazil are the [largest](/wiki/List_of_countries_by_number_of_military_and_paramilitary_personnel "List of countries by number of military and paramilitary personnel") in Latin America by active personnel and the largest in terms of military equipment.[[252]](#cite_note-256) It consists of the [Brazilian Army](/wiki/Brazilian_Army "Brazilian Army") (including the [Army Aviation Command](/wiki/Brazilian_Army_Aviation "Brazilian Army Aviation")), the [Brazilian Navy](/wiki/Brazilian_Navy "Brazilian Navy") (including the [Marine Corps](/wiki/Brazilian_Marine_Corps "Brazilian Marine Corps") and [Naval Aviation](/wiki/Brazilian_Naval_Aviation "Brazilian Naval Aviation")) and the [Brazilian Air Force](/wiki/Brazilian_Air_Force "Brazilian Air Force"). Brazil's [conscription policy](/wiki/Conscription_in_Brazil "Conscription in Brazil") gives it one of the world's largest military forces, estimated at more than 1.6 million [reservists](/wiki/Military_Reserve "Military Reserve") annually.[[253]](#cite_note-257) The Air Force is the largest in Latin America and has about 700 crewed aircraft in service and effective about 67,000 personnel.[[254]](#cite_note-258)

Numbering close to 236,000 active personnel,[[255]](#cite_note-259) the Brazilian Army has the largest number of armored vehicles in South America, including armored transports and [tanks](/wiki/Battle_tank "Battle tank").[[256]](#cite_note-militarypower1-260) The states' [Military Police](/wiki/Military_Police_(Brazil) "Military Police (Brazil)") and the [Military Firefighters Corps](/wiki/Military_Firefighters_Corps "Military Firefighters Corps") are designated as auxiliary forces of the army by the constitution, but are under the control of each state's governor.[[21]](#cite_note-Constituição-25)

Brazil's navy once operated some of the most powerful warships in the world with the two [*Minas Geraes*-class](/wiki/Minas_Geraes-class_battleship "Minas Geraes-class battleship") [dreadnoughts](/wiki/Dreadnought "Dreadnought"), sparking a [naval arms race between Argentina, Brazil, and Chile](/wiki/South_American_dreadnought_race "South American dreadnought race").[[257]](#cite_note-261) Today, it is a [green water](/wiki/Green-water_navy "Green-water navy") force and has a group of specialized elite in retaking ships and naval facilities, [GRUMEC](/wiki/GRUMEC "GRUMEC"), unit specially trained to protect Brazilian oil platforms along its coast.[[258]](#cite_note-262) As of 2022[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Brazil&action=edit), it is the only navy in Latin America that operates a [helicopter carrier](/wiki/Helicopter_carrier "Helicopter carrier"), [NAM *Atlântico*](/wiki/Brazilian_aircraft_carrier_Atl%C3%A2ntico "Brazilian aircraft carrier Atlântico") and one of twelve navies in the world to operate or have one under construction.[[259]](#cite_note-Poder_Naval-263)

### Law enforcement and crime

Main articles: [Law enforcement in Brazil](/wiki/Law_enforcement_in_Brazil "Law enforcement in Brazil") and [Crime in Brazil](/wiki/Crime_in_Brazil "Crime in Brazil")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/52/Sede_da_Pol%C3%ADcia_Federal.jpg/250px-Sede_da_Pol%C3%ADcia_Federal.jpg)](/wiki/File:Sede_da_Pol%C3%ADcia_Federal.jpg)

Headquarters of the Federal Police of Brazil in Brasília

In Brazil, the constitution establishes six different police agencies for law enforcement: [Federal Police Department](/wiki/Federal_Police_Department "Federal Police Department"), [Federal Highway Police](/wiki/Federal_Highway_Police_(Brazil) "Federal Highway Police (Brazil)"), [Federal Railroad Police](/wiki/Federal_Railroad_Police "Federal Railroad Police"), Federal, District and State Penal Police (included by the Constitutional Amendment No. 104, of 2019), [Military Police](/wiki/Military_Police_(Brazil) "Military Police (Brazil)") and [Civil Police](/wiki/Civil_Police_(Brazil) "Civil Police (Brazil)"). Of these, the former three are affiliated with federal authorities, the latter two are subordinate to state governments and the Penal Police can be subordinated to the federal or state/district government. All police forces are overseen by the executive branch of the federal or state governments.[[21]](#cite_note-Constituição-25) The [National Public Security Force](/wiki/National_Public_Security_Force "National Public Security Force") also can act in public disorder situations arising anywhere in the country.[[260]](#cite_note-264)

The country has high levels of violent crime, such as gun violence and homicides. In 2022, the [United Nations Office on Drugs and Crime](/wiki/United_Nations_Office_on_Drugs_and_Crime "United Nations Office on Drugs and Crime") (UNODC) estimated an intentional homicide rate of 21.1 per 100,000 inhabitants.[[261]](#cite_note-265) The number considered acceptable by the [World Health Organization](/wiki/World_Health_Organization "World Health Organization") (WHO) is about 10 homicides per 100,000 inhabitants.[[262]](#cite_note-266) In 2025, Brazil recorded 34,086 homicides, down from 38,374 in 2024,[[263]](#cite_note-:0-267) and from a record 63,880 in 2017.[[264]](#cite_note-268) Homicide rates vary regionally. While in [São Paulo](/wiki/S%C3%A3o_Paulo_(state) "São Paulo (state)") the homicide rate registered in 2025 was 5.4 per 100,000 inhabitants, in [Ceará](/wiki/Cear%C3%A1 "Ceará") it was 32.6 per 100,000 inhabitants.[[263]](#cite_note-:0-267) The national homicide rate for 2025 was 16 per 100,000 inhabitants, the lowest in over a decade.[[263]](#cite_note-:0-267)

Brazil also has high levels of incarceration. It had the third largest prison population in the world of approximately 909,067 prisoners in 2024, which put it only behind the United States (1,808,100) and China (1,690,000).[[265]](#cite_note-269) The high number of prisoners eventually overloaded the Brazilian prison system, leading to a shortfall of about 200,000 accommodations.[[266]](#cite_note-270)

### Human rights

See also: [Human rights in Brazil](/wiki/Human_rights_in_Brazil "Human rights in Brazil")

[Human rights in Brazil](/wiki/Human_rights_in_Brazil "Human rights in Brazil") include the [right to life](/wiki/Right_to_life "Right to life") and [freedom of speech](/wiki/Freedom_of_speech "Freedom of speech"); and condemnation of slavery and torture. The nation ratified the [American Convention on Human Rights](/wiki/American_Convention_on_Human_Rights "American Convention on Human Rights").[[267]](#cite_note-271) The 2017 [Freedom in the World](/wiki/Freedom_in_the_World "Freedom in the World") report by [Freedom House](/wiki/Freedom_House "Freedom House") gives Brazil a score of "2" for both political rights and civil liberties; "1" represents the most free, and "7", the least.[[268]](#cite_note-272) According to [UNESCO](/wiki/UNESCO "UNESCO"), "Brazil promotes a vast array of actions for the advancement and defense of human rights, even though it faces enormous social and economic inequalities".[[269]](#cite_note-273) Same-sex couples in Brazil have held nationwide [marriage rights](/wiki/Same-sex_marriage_in_Brazil "Same-sex marriage in Brazil") since May 2013.[[270]](#cite_note-edition.cnn.com-274)

## Economy

Main article: [Economy of Brazil](/wiki/Economy_of_Brazil "Economy of Brazil")

See also: [Brazilian real](/wiki/Brazilian_real "Brazilian real"), [Agriculture in Brazil](/wiki/Agriculture_in_Brazil "Agriculture in Brazil"), [Mining in Brazil](/wiki/Mining_in_Brazil "Mining in Brazil"), and [Industry in Brazil](/wiki/Industry_in_Brazil "Industry in Brazil")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/73/Marginal_Pinheiros_e_Jockey_Club.jpg/250px-Marginal_Pinheiros_e_Jockey_Club.jpg)](/wiki/File:Marginal_Pinheiros_e_Jockey_Club.jpg)

[São Paulo](/wiki/S%C3%A3o_Paulo "São Paulo") is considered the main financial center of Brazil

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b8/Sao_Paulo_Stock_Exchange.jpg/250px-Sao_Paulo_Stock_Exchange.jpg)](/wiki/File:Sao_Paulo_Stock_Exchange.jpg)

B3, the largest stock exchange of Latin America by market capitalization[[271]](#cite_note-275)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/bd/Soja_Tangar%C3%A1.jpg/250px-Soja_Tangar%C3%A1.jpg)](/wiki/File:Soja_Tangar%C3%A1.jpg)

Soybean crop in [Tangará da Serra](/wiki/Tangar%C3%A1_da_Serra "Tangará da Serra"), [Mato Grosso](/wiki/Mato_Grosso "Mato Grosso")[[272]](#cite_note-exportaçãoagrícola-276)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/56/10_03_2022_Opera%C3%A7%C3%A3o_Repatria%C3%A7%C3%A3o_-_Chegada_dos_Brasileiros_e_Estrangeiros_provenientes_da_Ucr%C3%A2nia_%2851931465965%29.jpg/250px-10_03_2022_Opera%C3%A7%C3%A3o_Repatria%C3%A7%C3%A3o_-_Chegada_dos_Brasileiros_e_Estrangeiros_provenientes_da_Ucr%C3%A2nia_%2851931465965%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:10_03_2022_Opera%C3%A7%C3%A3o_Repatria%C3%A7%C3%A3o_-_Chegada_dos_Brasileiros_e_Estrangeiros_provenientes_da_Ucr%C3%A2nia_(51931465965).jpg)

The [C-390](/wiki/Embraer_C-390_Millennium "Embraer C-390 Millennium"), developed by [Embraer](/wiki/Embraer "Embraer"), the third largest producer of civil aircraft, after [Boeing](/wiki/Boeing "Boeing") and [Airbus](/wiki/Airbus "Airbus")[[273]](#cite_note-277)

Brazil is a [developing country](/wiki/Developing_country "Developing country") with an [upper-middle income](/wiki/List_of_countries_by_GNI_(nominal)_per_capita#Upper-middle_income_group "List of countries by GNI (nominal) per capita") [mixed](/wiki/Mixed_economy "Mixed economy") [market economy](/wiki/Market_economy "Market economy") that is rich in natural resources.[[274]](#cite_note-278) It has the largest national economy in Latin America, the [tenth largest economy](/wiki/List_of_countries_by_GDP_(nominal) "List of countries by GDP (nominal)") in the world by nominal GDP, and the [eighth largest](/wiki/List_of_countries_by_GDP_(PPP) "List of countries by GDP (PPP)") by [PPP](/wiki/Purchasing_power_parity "Purchasing power parity").[[275]](#cite_note-279) After rapid growth in preceding decades, Brazil [entered a recession in 2014](/wiki/2014%E2%80%932016_Brazilian_economic_recession "2014–2016 Brazilian economic recession") amid a political corruption scandal and nationwide protests; in 2024, the economy began showing consistent significant growth.[[276]](#cite_note-280) Brazil has a [labor force](/wiki/Labor_force "Labor force") of roughly 100 million, which is the world's [fifth largest](/wiki/List_of_countries_by_labour_force "List of countries by labour force").[[277]](#cite_note-281) Its [foreign exchange reserves](/wiki/Foreign_exchange_reserves "Foreign exchange reserves") are the [tenth-highest](/wiki/List_of_countries_by_foreign_exchange_reserves "List of countries by foreign exchange reserves") in the world.[[278]](#cite_note-282) The [B3](/wiki/B3_(stock_exchange) "B3 (stock exchange)") in São Paulo is the [largest stock exchange of Latin America by market capitalization](/wiki/List_of_stock_exchanges "List of stock exchanges"). Roughly one-fifth of Brazilians live in poverty: about 3.8% of the total population lives at $3.00 a day,[[279]](#cite_note-283) while about 23% live at $8.30 a day.[[280]](#cite_note-284) Brazil's economy suffers from [endemic corruption](/wiki/Corruption_in_Brazil "Corruption in Brazil") and [high income inequality](/wiki/Income_inequality_in_Brazil "Income inequality in Brazil").[[281]](#cite_note-285) The [Brazilian real](/wiki/Brazilian_real "Brazilian real") is the national currency.

Brazil's diversified economy includes agriculture, industry and a wide range of services.[[282]](#cite_note-BansalPhatak2009-286) The large service sector accounts for about 72.7% of total GDP, followed by the industrial sector (20.7%), while the [agriculture sector](/wiki/Agriculture_in_Brazil "Agriculture in Brazil") is by far the smallest, making up 6.6% of total GDP.[[283]](#cite_note-287)

Brazil is one of the [largest producers of various agricultural commodities](/wiki/List_of_largest_producing_countries_of_agricultural_commodities "List of largest producing countries of agricultural commodities"),[[284]](#cite_note-288) and also has a large [cooperative](/wiki/Cooperative "Cooperative") sector that provides 50% of the food in the country.[[285]](#cite_note-289) It has been the world's largest [producer of coffee](/wiki/Coffee_production_in_Brazil "Coffee production in Brazil") for the last 150 years[[34]](#cite_note-Neilson102-38) and is the world's largest producer of sugarcane, soy, coffee and oranges; is one of the top five producers of maize, cotton, lemon, tobacco, pineapple, banana, beans, coconut, watermelon and papaya; and is one of the top 10 world producers of cocoa, cashew, mango, rice, tomato, sorghum, tangerine, avocado, persimmon, and guava, among others. Regarding livestock, it is one of the five largest producers of chicken meat, beef, pork and cow's milk in the world.[[286]](#cite_note-290)

In the [mining sector](/wiki/Mining_in_Brazil "Mining in Brazil"), Brazil is among the largest producers of iron ore, copper, gold,[[287]](#cite_note-291) bauxite, manganese, tin, [niobium](/wiki/Niobium "Niobium"),[[288]](#cite_note-292) and nickel. In terms of precious stones, Brazil is the world's largest producer of [amethyst](/wiki/Amethyst "Amethyst"), [topaz](/wiki/Topaz "Topaz"), [agate](/wiki/Agate "Agate") and one of the main producers of [tourmaline](/wiki/Tourmaline "Tourmaline"), [emerald](/wiki/Emerald "Emerald"), [aquamarine](/wiki/Aquamarine_(gemstone) "Aquamarine (gemstone)"), [garnet](/wiki/Garnet "Garnet") and [opal](/wiki/Opal "Opal").[[289]](#cite_note-293)[[290]](#cite_note-294) The country is a major exporter of soy, iron ore, pulp (cellulose), maize, beef, chicken meat, soybean meal, sugar, coffee, tobacco, cotton, orange juice, footwear, airplanes, cars, vehicle parts, gold, ethanol and semi-finished iron, among other products.[[291]](#cite_note-295)[[292]](#cite_note-296)

Brazil is the world's [24th-largest exporter](/wiki/List_of_countries_by_exports "List of countries by exports") and [26th-largest importer](/wiki/List_of_countries_by_imports "List of countries by imports") as of 2021[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Brazil&action=edit).[[293]](#cite_note-297)[[294]](#cite_note-298) China is its largest trading partner, accounting for 32% of the total trade. Other large trading partners include the United States, Argentina, the Netherlands and Canada.[[295]](#cite_note-299) Its [automotive industry](/wiki/Automotive_industry_in_Brazil "Automotive industry in Brazil") is the [eighth-largest](/wiki/List_of_countries_by_motor_vehicle_production "List of countries by motor vehicle production") in the world.[[296]](#cite_note-300) In the [food industry](/wiki/Food_industry "Food industry"), Brazil was the second-largest exporter of processed foods in the world in 2019.[[297]](#cite_note-301) The country was the second-largest producer of [pulp](/wiki/Pulp_(paper) "Pulp (paper)") in the world and the eighth-largest producer of paper in 2016.[[298]](#cite_note-302) In the [footwear industry](/wiki/Footwear_industry "Footwear industry"), Brazil was the fourth-largest producer in 2019.[[299]](#cite_note-303) It was also the ninth-largest producer of steel in the world.[[300]](#cite_note-304)[[301]](#cite_note-305)[[302]](#cite_note-306) In 2018, the [chemical industry](/wiki/Chemical_industry "Chemical industry") of Brazil was the eighth-largest in the world.[[303]](#cite_note-307)[[304]](#cite_note-308)[[305]](#cite_note-309) Although it was among the five largest world producers in 2013, Brazil's textile industry is very little integrated into world trade.[[306]](#cite_note-310)

The tertiary sector (trade and services) represented 75.8% of the country's GDP in 2018, according to the IBGE. The service sector was responsible for 60% of GDP and trade for 13%. It covers commerce, transport, education, social and health services, research and development, sports activities, etc.[[307]](#cite_note-311)[[308]](#cite_note-312) Micro and small businesses represent 30% of the country's GDP. In the commercial sector they represent 53% of the GDP within the activities of the sector.[[309]](#cite_note-313)

### Tourism

Main article: [Tourism in Brazil](/wiki/Tourism_in_Brazil "Tourism in Brazil")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c4/Iguazu_Cataratas2.jpg/250px-Iguazu_Cataratas2.jpg)](/wiki/File:Iguazu_Cataratas2.jpg)

[Iguaçu National Park](/wiki/Igua%C3%A7u_National_Park "Iguaçu National Park") in [Paraná](/wiki/Paran%C3%A1_(state) "Paraná (state)")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/55/Len%C3%A7%C3%B3is_Maranhenses_2018_%28cropped%29.jpg/250px-Len%C3%A7%C3%B3is_Maranhenses_2018_%28cropped%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Len%C3%A7%C3%B3is_Maranhenses_2018_(cropped).jpg)

[Lençóis Maranhenses](/wiki/Len%C3%A7%C3%B3is_Maranhenses "Lençóis Maranhenses") in [Maranhão](/wiki/Maranh%C3%A3o "Maranhão")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/ef/6_ILHA_DAS_COUVES_-_UBATUBA.jpg/250px-6_ILHA_DAS_COUVES_-_UBATUBA.jpg)](/wiki/File:6_ILHA_DAS_COUVES_-_UBATUBA.jpg)

[Ilha das Couves](/wiki/Ilha_das_Couves "Ilha das Couves") in [Ubatuba](/wiki/Ubatuba "Ubatuba"), [São Paulo](/wiki/S%C3%A3o_Paulo_(state) "São Paulo (state)")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/ae/Christ_on_Corcovado_mountain.JPG/250px-Christ_on_Corcovado_mountain.JPG)](/wiki/File:Christ_on_Corcovado_mountain.JPG)

[Christ the Redeemer](/wiki/Christ_the_Redeemer_(statue) "Christ the Redeemer (statue)") in [Rio de Janeiro](/wiki/Rio_de_Janeiro "Rio de Janeiro")

Tourism in Brazil is a growing sector and key to the economies of several regions of the country. The country had 6.36 million visitors in 2015, ranking in terms of the international tourist arrivals as the main destination in South America and second in Latin America after Mexico.[[310]](#cite_note-314) Revenues from international tourists reached US$6 billion in 2010, showing a recovery from the [2008–2009 economic crisis](/wiki/Late-2000s_recession "Late-2000s recession").[[311]](#cite_note-UNWTO2011-315) Historical records of 5.4 million visitors and US$6.8 billion in receipts were reached in 2011.[[312]](#cite_note-MT1_2011-316)[[313]](#cite_note-MT2_2011-317) In the list of world tourist destinations, in 2018, Brazil was the 48th most visited country, with 6.6 million tourists (and revenues of 5.9 billion dollars).[[314]](#cite_note-318)

Natural areas are its most popular tourism product, a combination of [ecotourism](/wiki/Ecotourism "Ecotourism") with leisure and recreation, mainly sun and beach, and adventure travel, as well as cultural tourism. Among the most popular destinations are the Amazon Rainforest, beaches and dunes in the [Northeast Region](/wiki/Northeast_Region,_Brazil "Northeast Region, Brazil"), the [Pantanal](/wiki/Pantanal "Pantanal") in the [Center-West Region](/wiki/Center-West_Region,_Brazil "Center-West Region, Brazil"), beaches at [Rio de Janeiro](/wiki/Rio_de_Janeiro_(state) "Rio de Janeiro (state)") and [Santa Catarina](/wiki/Santa_Catarina_(state) "Santa Catarina (state)"), cultural tourism in [Minas Gerais](/wiki/Minas_Gerais "Minas Gerais") and business trips to [São Paulo](/wiki/S%C3%A3o_Paulo "São Paulo").[[315]](#cite_note-Palhares2012-319)

In terms of the 2024 [Travel and Tourism Competitiveness Index](/wiki/Travel_and_Tourism_Development_Index "Travel and Tourism Development Index") (TTCI), which is a measurement of the factors that make it attractive to develop business in the travel and tourism industry of individual countries, Brazil ranked in the 26th place at the world's level, third in the Americas, after Canada and United States.[[316]](#cite_note-320) [Domestic tourism](/wiki/Domestic_tourism "Domestic tourism") is a key market segment for the tourism industry in Brazil. In 2005, 51 million Brazilian nationals made ten times more trips than foreign tourists and spent five times more money than their international counterparts.[[317]](#cite_note-FIPE007-321) The main destination states in 2023 were [São Paulo](/wiki/S%C3%A3o_Paulo_(state) "São Paulo (state)"), [Rio de Janeiro](/wiki/Rio_de_Janeiro_(state) "Rio de Janeiro (state)"), and [Rio Grande do Sul](/wiki/Rio_Grande_do_Sul "Rio Grande do Sul").[[318]](#cite_note-322)[[319]](#cite_note-323) The main source of tourists for the entire country is São Paulo state.[[320]](#cite_note-324) In terms of tourism revenues, the top earners by state were [São Paulo](/wiki/S%C3%A3o_Paulo_(state) "São Paulo (state)") and [Bahia](/wiki/Bahia "Bahia").[[321]](#cite_note-325) For 2005, the three main trip purposes were visiting friends and family (53.1%), sun and beach (40.8%), and cultural tourism (12.5%).[[322]](#cite_note-FIPE006-326)

### Science and technology

Main article: [Science and technology in Brazil](/wiki/Science_and_technology_in_Brazil "Science and technology in Brazil")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f2/Vls1-mockup-test.jpg/250px-Vls1-mockup-test.jpg)](/wiki/File:Vls1-mockup-test.jpg)

[VLS-1](/wiki/VLS-1 "VLS-1") at the [Alcântara Launch Center](/wiki/Alc%C3%A2ntara_Launch_Center "Alcântara Launch Center") of the Brazilian Space Agency, in [Alcântara, Maranhão](/wiki/Alc%C3%A2ntara,_Maranh%C3%A3o "Alcântara, Maranhão")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b6/Ministro_participa_da_inaugura%C3%A7%C3%A3o_do_acelerador_de_part%C3%ADculas_Sirius._%2830970744907%29.jpg/250px-Ministro_participa_da_inaugura%C3%A7%C3%A3o_do_acelerador_de_part%C3%ADculas_Sirius._%2830970744907%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Ministro_participa_da_inaugura%C3%A7%C3%A3o_do_acelerador_de_part%C3%ADculas_Sirius._(30970744907).jpg)

[Sirius](/wiki/Sirius_(synchrotron_light_source) "Sirius (synchrotron light source)"), a [diffraction-limited storage ring](/wiki/Diffraction-limited_storage_ring "Diffraction-limited storage ring") [synchrotron light source](/wiki/Synchrotron_light_source "Synchrotron light source") at the [Laboratório Nacional de Luz Síncrotron](/wiki/Laborat%C3%B3rio_Nacional_de_Luz_S%C3%ADncrotron "Laboratório Nacional de Luz Síncrotron"), in [Campinas](/wiki/Campinas "Campinas"), São Paulo

Technological research in Brazil is largely carried out in public universities and research institutes, with the majority of funding for basic research coming from various government agencies.[[323]](#cite_note-DevelopmentStaff2006v-327) Brazil's most esteemed technological hubs are the [Oswaldo Cruz Institute](/wiki/Funda%C3%A7%C3%A3o_Oswaldo_Cruz "Fundação Oswaldo Cruz"), the [Butantan Institute](/wiki/Instituto_Butantan "Instituto Butantan"), the Air Force's [Aerospace Technical Center](/wiki/Brazilian_General_Command_for_Aerospace_Technology "Brazilian General Command for Aerospace Technology"), the [Brazilian Agricultural Research Corporation](/wiki/Empresa_Brasileira_de_Pesquisa_Agropecu%C3%A1ria "Empresa Brasileira de Pesquisa Agropecuária") and the [National Institute for Space Research](/wiki/National_Institute_for_Space_Research "National Institute for Space Research").[[324]](#cite_note-Scientific2010f-328)[[325]](#cite_note-HarveySmid2010-329)

The [Brazilian Space Agency](/wiki/Brazilian_Space_Agency "Brazilian Space Agency") has the most advanced space program in Latin America, with significant resources to launch vehicles, and manufacture of satellites.[[326]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECrocittiVallance2012[httpsbooksgooglecombooksidvP9jHaoL_s4CpgPA628_628]-330) The country [develops submarines](/wiki/Submarine_Development_Program "Submarine Development Program") and aircraft, as well as being involved in space research, having a Vehicle Launch Center Light and being the only country in the Southern Hemisphere to integrate a team building the well-known [International Space Station](/wiki/International_Space_Station "International Space Station") (ISS).[[327]](#cite_note-NASA-331)

The country is also a pioneer in the search for oil in deep water, from where it extracts 73% of its reserves. Uranium is enriched at the [Resende Nuclear Fuel Factory](/wiki/Resende_Nuclear_Fuel_Factory "Resende Nuclear Fuel Factory"), mostly for research purposes (as Brazil obtains 88% of its electricity from [hydroelectricity](/wiki/Hydroelectricity "Hydroelectricity"))[[328]](#cite_note-332) and the country's [first nuclear submarine](/wiki/Brazilian_submarine_%C3%81lvaro_Alberto "Brazilian submarine Álvaro Alberto") is expected to be launched in 2029.[[329]](#cite_note-333)

Brazil is one of the three countries in Latin America[[330]](#cite_note-334) with an operational [Synchrotron](/wiki/Synchrotron "Synchrotron") Laboratory, a research facility on physics, chemistry, material science and life sciences, and Brazil is the only Latin American country to have a semiconductor company with its own [fabrication plant](/wiki/Semiconductor_fabrication_plant "Semiconductor fabrication plant"), the [CEITEC](/wiki/CEITEC "CEITEC").[[331]](#cite_note-335) According to the Global Information Technology Report 2009–2010 of the World Economic Forum, Brazil is the world's 61st largest developer of information technology.[[332]](#cite_note-336) Brazil was ranked 52st in the [Global Innovation Index](/wiki/Global_Innovation_Index "Global Innovation Index") in 2025, up from 66th in 2019.[[333]](#cite_note-337)[[334]](#cite_note-338)[[335]](#cite_note-339)

Among the most renowned Brazilian inventors are priests [Bartolomeu de Gusmão](/wiki/Bartolomeu_de_Gusm%C3%A3o "Bartolomeu de Gusmão"), [Landell de Moura](/wiki/Landell_de_Moura "Landell de Moura") and Francisco João de Azevedo, besides [Alberto Santos-Dumont](/wiki/Alberto_Santos-Dumont "Alberto Santos-Dumont"),[[336]](#cite_note-340) [Evaristo Conrado Engelberg](/wiki/Evaristo_Conrado_Engelberg "Evaristo Conrado Engelberg"),[[337]](#cite_note-patentbritish-341) [Manuel Dias de Abreu](/wiki/Manuel_Dias_de_Abreu "Manuel Dias de Abreu"),[[338]](#cite_note-342) [Andreas Pavel](/wiki/Andreas_Pavel "Andreas Pavel")[[339]](#cite_note-343) and Nélio José Nicolai.[[340]](#cite_note-344) Brazilian science is represented by the likes of [César Lattes](/wiki/C%C3%A9sar_Lattes "César Lattes") (Brazilian physicist Pathfinder of *[Pi Meson](/wiki/Pion "Pion")*),[[341]](#cite_note-Lattes-345) [Mário Schenberg](/wiki/M%C3%A1rio_Schenberg "Mário Schenberg") (considered the greatest theoretical physicist of Brazil),[[342]](#cite_note-346) [José Leite Lopes](/wiki/Jos%C3%A9_Leite_Lopes "José Leite Lopes") (the only Brazilian physicist holder of the *UNESCO Science Prize*),[[343]](#cite_note-347) [Artur Avila](/wiki/Artur_Avila "Artur Avila") (the first Latin American winner of the [Fields Medal](/wiki/Fields_Medal "Fields Medal"))[[344]](#cite_note-348) and [Fritz Müller](/wiki/Fritz_M%C3%BCller "Fritz Müller") (pioneer in factual support of the theory of evolution by [Charles Darwin](/wiki/Charles_Darwin "Charles Darwin")).[[345]](#cite_note-West,_David_A_2003-349)

### Energy

Main article: [Energy in Brazil](/wiki/Energy_in_Brazil "Energy in Brazil")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d2/Itaipu_geral.jpg/250px-Itaipu_geral.jpg)](/wiki/File:Itaipu_geral.jpg)

The [Itaipu Dam](/wiki/Itaipu_Dam "Itaipu Dam") on the [Paraná River](/wiki/Paran%C3%A1_River "Paraná River"), the second largest of the world. [Brazilian energy matrix](/wiki/Energy_policy_of_Brazil "Energy policy of Brazil") is one of the [cleanest](/wiki/Clean_energy "Clean energy") in the world

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/86/Energia_Eolica.jpg/250px-Energia_Eolica.jpg)](/wiki/File:Energia_Eolica.jpg)

[Wind farm](/wiki/Wind_farm "Wind farm") in [Parnaíba](/wiki/Parna%C3%ADba "Parnaíba"), [Piauí](/wiki/Piau%C3%AD "Piauí"). Brazil is one of the 5 largest producers of wind energy in the world

Brazil is the world's [eleventh-largest](/wiki/List_of_countries_by_energy_consumption_and_production "List of countries by energy consumption and production") energy consumer.[[346]](#cite_note-350) Much of its energy comes from [renewable sources](/wiki/Renewable_energy "Renewable energy"), particularly [hydroelectricity](/wiki/Hydroelectricity "Hydroelectricity") and [ethanol](/wiki/Ethanol "Ethanol"); the [Itaipu Dam](/wiki/Itaipu_Dam "Itaipu Dam") is the world's largest [hydroelectric plant](/wiki/Hydroelectricity "Hydroelectricity") by energy generation,[[347]](#cite_note-SchmidtOnyango2011-351) and the country has other large plants such as [Belo Monte](/wiki/Belo_Monte_Dam "Belo Monte Dam") and [Tucuruí](/wiki/Tucuru%C3%AD_Dam "Tucuruí Dam"). The first car with an ethanol engine was produced in 1978 and the first airplane engine running on ethanol in 2005.[[348]](#cite_note-OECDDevelopment2001-352)

At the end of 2021 Brazil was the 2nd country in the world in terms of installed [hydroelectric power](/wiki/Hydroelectric_power "Hydroelectric power") (109.4 GW) and [biomass](/wiki/Biomass "Biomass") (15.8 GW), the 7th country in the world in terms of installed [wind power](/wiki/Wind_power "Wind power") (21.1 GW) and the 14th country in the world in terms of installed [solar power](/wiki/Solar_power "Solar power") (13.0 GW)—on track to also become one of the top 10 in the world in solar energy.[[349]](#cite_note-353) At the end of 2024, Brazil was the 4th largest producer of wind energy in the world (107.8 TWh), behind only China, the United States and Germany, and the 5th largest producer of solar energy in the world (74.7 TWh).[[350]](#cite_note-ember-354)

The main characteristic of the Brazilian energy matrix is that it is much more renewable than that of the world. While in 2019, the world matrix was only 14% made up of renewable energy, Brazil's was at 45%. Petroleum and oil products made up 34.3% of the matrix; sugar cane derivatives, 18%; hydraulic energy, 12.4%; natural gas, 12.2%; firewood and charcoal, 8.8%; varied renewable energies, 7%; mineral coal, 5.3%; nuclear, 1.4%, and other non-renewable energies, 0.6%.[[351]](#cite_note-Matriz_Energética_e_Elétrica-355)

In the electric energy matrix, the difference between Brazil and the world is even greater: while the world only had 25% of renewable electric energy in 2019, Brazil had 83%. The Brazilian electric matrix was composed of: hydraulic energy, 64.9%; biomass, 8.4%; wind energy, 8.6%; solar energy, 1%; natural gas, 9.3%; oil products, 2%; nuclear, 2.5%; coal and derivatives, 3.3%.[[351]](#cite_note-Matriz_Energética_e_Elétrica-355) Brazil has the largest electricity sector in Latin America. Its capacity at the end of 2021 was 181,532 MW.[[352]](#cite_note-Ministério_de_Minas_e_Energia-356)

The Brazilian government has embarked on a program over the decades to reduce dependence on imported oil, which previously accounted for more than 70% of the country's oil needs. Brazil became self-sufficient in oil in 2006–2007. In 2021, the country closed the year as the 7th oil producer in the world, with an average of close to three million barrels per day, becoming an exporter of the product.[[353]](#cite_note-357)[[354]](#cite_note-358)

### Transportation

Main article: [Transport in Brazil](/wiki/Transport_in_Brazil "Transport in Brazil")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e1/Sao_paulo_airport_terminal_3.jpg/250px-Sao_paulo_airport_terminal_3.jpg)](/wiki/File:Sao_paulo_airport_terminal_3.jpg)

Terminal 3 of the [São Paulo–Guarulhos International Airport](/wiki/S%C3%A3o_Paulo%E2%80%93Guarulhos_International_Airport "São Paulo–Guarulhos International Airport"), the [busiest airport in Latin America](/wiki/List_of_the_busiest_airports_in_Latin_America "List of the busiest airports in Latin America")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a9/Rodovia_Dutra_-_panoramio.jpg/250px-Rodovia_Dutra_-_panoramio.jpg)](/wiki/File:Rodovia_Dutra_-_panoramio.jpg)

[BR-116](/wiki/BR-116 "BR-116") in [São José dos Campos](/wiki/S%C3%A3o_Jos%C3%A9_dos_Campos "São José dos Campos"), [São Paulo](/wiki/S%C3%A3o_Paulo_(state) "São Paulo (state)"), the longest [highway](/wiki/Brazilian_Highway_System "Brazilian Highway System") in the country,[[355]](#cite_note-359) with 4,542 km (2,822 mi) of extension[[356]](#cite_note-Extensão-360)

Brazilian roads are the primary carriers of freight and passenger traffic. The [road system](/wiki/Brazilian_Highway_System "Brazilian Highway System") totaled 1,720,000 km (1,068,758 mi) in 2019.[[357]](#cite_note-361) The total of paved roads increased from 35,496 km (22,056 mi) in 1967 to 215,000 km (133,595 mi) in 2018.[[358]](#cite_note-362)

Brazil's [railway system](/wiki/Rail_transport_in_Brazil "Rail transport in Brazil") has been declining since 1945, when emphasis shifted to highway construction. The country's total railway track length was 30,576 km (18,999 mi) in 2015,[[359]](#cite_note-363) as compared with 31,848 km (19,789 mi) in 1970, making it the [ninth largest network](/wiki/List_of_countries_by_rail_transport_network_size "List of countries by rail transport network size") in the world. Most of the railway system belonged to the [Federal Railroad Network Corporation](/wiki/RFFSA "RFFSA") (RFFSA), which was privatized in 2007.[[360]](#cite_note-364) The [São Paulo Metro](/wiki/S%C3%A3o_Paulo_Metro "São Paulo Metro") began operating on 14 September 1974 as the first underground transit system in Brazil.[[361]](#cite_note-365)

There are about 2,500 airports in Brazil, including landing fields: the second-largest number in the world, after the United States.[[362]](#cite_note-366) [São Paulo–Guarulhos International Airport](/wiki/S%C3%A3o_Paulo%E2%80%93Guarulhos_International_Airport "São Paulo–Guarulhos International Airport"), near São Paulo, is the largest and busiest airport with nearly 43 million passengers annually, while handling the vast majority of commercial traffic for the country.[[363]](#cite_note-Palhares2012x-367)[[364]](#cite_note-368)

For freight transport, waterways are of importance. The [industrial zones of Manaus](/wiki/Free_Economic_Zone_of_Manaus "Free Economic Zone of Manaus") can be reached only by means of the Solimões–Amazonas waterway (3,250 kilometers or 2,020 miles in length, with a minimum depth of six meters or 20 feet). The country also has 50,000 kilometers (31,000 miles) of waterways.[[365]](#cite_note-2012ciagini-369) Coastal shipping links widely separated parts of the country. Bolivia and Paraguay have been given free ports at [Santos](/wiki/Santos,_S%C3%A3o_Paulo "Santos, São Paulo"). Of the 36 deep-water ports, [Santos](/wiki/Port_of_Santos "Port of Santos"), [Itajaí](/wiki/Port_of_Itaja%C3%AD "Port of Itajaí"), [Rio Grande](/wiki/Port_of_Rio_Grande "Port of Rio Grande"), [Paranaguá](/wiki/Port_of_Paranagu%C3%A1 "Port of Paranaguá"), [Rio de Janeiro](/wiki/Port_of_Rio_de_Janeiro "Port of Rio de Janeiro"), Sepetiba, [Vitória](/wiki/Port_of_Tubar%C3%A3o "Port of Tubarão"), [Suape](/wiki/Suape_Port "Suape Port"), [Manaus](/wiki/Port_of_Manaus "Port of Manaus") and [São Francisco do Sul](/wiki/Port_of_S%C3%A3o_Francisco_do_Sul "Port of São Francisco do Sul") are the most important.[[366]](#cite_note-370) Bulk carriers have to wait up to 18 days before being serviced; container ships take 36.3 hours on average.[[367]](#cite_note-371)

## Demographics

Main articles: [Demographics of Brazil](/wiki/Demographics_of_Brazil "Demographics of Brazil") and [Brazilians](/wiki/Brazilians "Brazilians")

See also: [Immigration to Brazil](/wiki/Immigration_to_Brazil "Immigration to Brazil") and [List of Brazilian states by population density](/wiki/List_of_Brazilian_states_by_population_density "List of Brazilian states by population density")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f1/Population_density_Brazil_2022.svg/250px-Population_density_Brazil_2022.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Population_density_Brazil_2022.svg)

Population density of Brazilian municipalities

According to the latest official projection, Brazil's estimated population was 213,421,037 on 1 July 2025—an increase from the figure of 203 million reported by the 2022 census.[[368]](#cite_note-372)[[369]](#cite_note-373) The population of Brazil, as recorded by the 2008 PNAD, was approximately 190 million[[370]](#cite_note-374) (22.31 inhabitants per square kilometer or 57.8/sq mi), with a ratio of men to women of 0.95:1[[371]](#cite_note-375) and 83.75% of the population defined as urban.[[372]](#cite_note-376) The population is heavily concentrated in the Southeast (89 million inhabitants) and Northeast (54.6 million inhabitants), while the two most extensive regions, the Center-West and the North, which together make up 64.12% of Brazilian territory, have a total of only 33.8 million inhabitants.

The first census in Brazil was carried out [in 1872](/wiki/1872_Brazilian_census "1872 Brazilian census") and recorded a population of 9,930,478.[[373]](#cite_note-377) From 1880 to 1930, four million Europeans arrived.[[374]](#cite_note-378) Brazil's population increased significantly between 1940 and 1970, because of a decline in the [mortality rate](/wiki/Mortality_rate "Mortality rate"), even though the [birth rate](/wiki/Birth_rate "Birth rate") underwent a slight decline. In the 1940s the annual population growth rate was 2.4%, rising to 3.0% in the 1950s and remaining at 2.9% in the 1960s, as life expectancy rose from 44 to 54 years[[375]](#cite_note-379) and to 72.6 years in 2007.[[376]](#cite_note-380) It has been steadily falling since the 1960s, from 3.04% per year between 1950 and 1960 to 1.05% in 2008 and is expected to fall to a negative value of –0.29% by 2050[[377]](#cite_note-381) thus completing the [demographic transition](/wiki/Demographic_transition "Demographic transition").[[378]](#cite_note-382) In 2022, the illiteracy rate was roughly 7%,[[379]](#cite_note-RF-383) a significant decline from 11.48% in 2008.[[380]](#cite_note-384) By comparison, in 1940, more than half the population (54%) was illiterate.[[379]](#cite_note-RF-383)

### Urbanization

Main article: [List of cities in Brazil by population](/wiki/List_of_cities_in_Brazil_by_population "List of cities in Brazil by population")

According to IBGE (Brazilian Institute of Geography and Statistics) urban areas already concentrate 84.35% of the population, while the Southeast region remains the most populated one, with over 80 million inhabitants.[[381]](#cite_note-385)
The largest urban agglomerations in Brazil are [São Paulo](/wiki/S%C3%A3o_Paulo "São Paulo"), [Rio de Janeiro](/wiki/Rio_de_Janeiro "Rio de Janeiro"), and [Belo Horizonte](/wiki/Belo_Horizonte "Belo Horizonte")—all in the Southeastern Region—with 21.1, 12.3, and 5.1 million inhabitants respectively.[[382]](#cite_note-concentrações_urbanas-386)[[383]](#cite_note-387)[[384]](#cite_note-388) The majority of state capitals are the largest cities in their states, except for [Vitória](/wiki/Vit%C3%B3ria,_Brazil "Vitória, Brazil"), the capital of [Espírito Santo](/wiki/Esp%C3%ADrito_Santo "Espírito Santo"), and [Florianópolis](/wiki/Florian%C3%B3polis "Florianópolis"), the capital of Santa Catarina.[[385]](#cite_note-389)
